coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot membranes as fluorescent sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Carrasco, Pedro Ma
García, Ignacio
Yate, Luis
Tena Zaera, Ramón
Cabañero, Germán
Grande, Hans J.
Ruiz, Virginia
coredata.dc:description: A simple and versatile method to produce filtration membranes with graphene quantum dots (GQD) and their excellent performance as turn-off fluorescence sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection in water is presented. Suitably functionalized GQD were immobilized on various types of membranes (nylon, cellulose and glass fiber) and the resulting GQD-modified membranes exhibited the characteristic fluorescence fingerprint of the GQD probes. The membrane fluorescence was quenched by highly toxic Cr (VI) ions due to selective coordination to GQD functional groups. The analytical performance of membranes was comparable to that of dispersed GQD fluorescent probes, displaying a fast and linear response to Cr (VI) concentration in the 1–500 μM range, a low detection limit of 190 nM, high sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity. Moreover, the membranes can be regenerated by simple washing with deionized water and reused, preserving their good sensing response after several Cr (VI) detection-washing cycles. Membrane sensing performance was very stable after being immersed in water for several days and filtering water through them.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) and interferents
Characterization of GQD and GQD-nylon membranes
Storage and operational stability of GQD-modified membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microscopy characterization of GQD: (A, C) TEM image and corresponding lateral size distribution of GQD. (B, D) AFM image with height profile and corresponding thickness distribution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Spectroscopic and optical characterization of GQD: (A) Raman spectra, (B) FTIR spectra, (C) UV–visible absorption, PL excitation and emission (λexc = 420 nm) spectra, (D) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Comparison of PL emission spectra (λexc = 420 nm) of GQD dispersed in water and immobilized in a nylon membrane. Inset: photographs of GQD aqueous solution (0.015 mg/mL) under natural and UV light. (B) PL intensity at 496 nm (λexc = 420 nm) and photographs of GQD-nylon membranes produced from GQD bathing solutions with different concentrations: (o) membranes in 0.1 M PBS pH 7, (•) membranes in 100 μM Cr (VI) and 0.1 M PBS pH 7. (C) GQD immobilized in different types of membranes under natural and UV light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) PL emission spectra of GQD-nylon membrane immersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 upon addition of increasing Cr (VI) concentrations (from top to bottom): 0, 57, 111, 163, 227, 294, 357, 416 and 500 μM. Inset: fluorescence plate with identical GQD-nylon membranes in solutions with different Cr (VI) concentrations under natural and UV light (from left to right): 57, 111, 163, 227, 294, 357, 416 and 500 μM. (B) Corresponding plot of (F0-F)/F at 496 nm versus Cr (VI) concentration: response of 0.015 mg/mL GQD solution (red), a single piece (green) and multiple pieces (blue) of GQD-nylon membrane. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0 at 496 nm) of GQD-nylon membranes immersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 containing different metal ions at the concentrations indicated in the figure. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Operational stability of GQD-nylon membranes as sensing platforms: plot of (F0-F)/F at 496 nm versus Cr (VI) concentration recorded after different immersion times (A) and after filtration of different volumes of DI water (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot membranes as fluorescent sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Division, IK4-CIDETEC, Paseo Miramón 196, E-20009 Donostia-San Sebastián, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CIC biomaGUNE, Paseo Miramón 192, E-20009 Donostia-San Sebastián, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and versatile method to produce filtration membranes with graphene quantum dots (GQD) and their excellent performance as turn-off fluorescence sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection in water is presented. Suitably functionalized GQD were immobilized on various types of membranes (nylon, cellulose and glass fiber) and the resulting GQD-modified membranes exhibited the characteristic fluorescence fingerprint of the GQD probes. The membrane fluorescence was quenched by highly toxic Cr (VI) ions due to selective coordination to GQD functional groups. The analytical performance of membranes was comparable to that of dispersed GQD fluorescent probes, displaying a fast and linear response to Cr (VI) concentration in the 1–500 μM range, a low detection limit of 190 nM, high sensitivity, reproducibility and selectivity. Moreover, the membranes can be regenerated by simple washing with deionized water and reused, preserving their good sensing response after several Cr (VI) detection-washing cycles. Membrane sensing performance was very stable after being immersed in water for several days and filtering water through them.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have emerged as a new class of fluorescent materials with tunable electronic and optical properties suitable for applications in many fields such as catalysis [1], energy conversion and storage [2], photovoltaics [3], bioimaging [4], lighting [5] or sensing [6]. Compared with organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQD are superior and versatile fluorophores due to its size- and composition-tunable emission, high photostability against bleaching and blinking, biocompatibility, excellent solubility and low toxicity [7].

These unique and interesting optical properties have triggered intensive use of GQD in analytical science for environmental and biological applications [8,9]. Thus, GQD with different size, crystallinity and functional groups have been tailored as fluorescent probes to selectively detect a number of analytes capable of quenching GQD fluorescence. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is based on photo-induced electron transfer from excited GQD to the analyte (normally an electron acceptor) and this “turn-off” sensing scheme has been applied to detect selectively numerous target analytes: from hazardous ions such as Hg2+ [10], Fe3+ [11], Cu2+ [12], Ni2+ [13] or ClO− [14] to different biomolecules such as glucose [15], fructose [16], melamine [17], among others.

However, these studies typically report novel synthetic routes of suitably functionalized GQD and prove their use as soluble fluorescent probes for turn-off selective sensing co-dispersed with the target analyte. In contrast, the use of GQD as fluorescent sensing probes immobilized on surfaces is comparatively scarce despite being more practical from the application's viewpoint. Thus, there have been only few recent attempts to develop GQD-based fluorescent solid sensing platforms. Zhang et al. immobilized GQD into a nanofibrous membrane by electrospinning the water-soluble GQD and polyvinyl alcohol, fabricating a dual-purpose fluorescence and electrochemical biosensing platform for H2O2 detection [18]. Later on, GQD were adsorbed on a polystyrenic anion-exchange resin and the resulting solid fluorescent sensor was applied to the selective detection of Fe3+ [19]. Regeneration of the sensor was achieved by separating Fe3+ with the complexing agent EDTA. Just recently, Chen et al. deposited GQD-chitosan-creatinine mixtures on silica slides and the resulting fluorescent solid films were applied to detect picric acid in water based on the quenched fluorescence of GQD [20].

In this paper, we report a simple and versatile method to produce GQD-containing membranes with an excellent performance as turn-off fluorescent sensing platforms for selective detection of Cr (VI). Determination of trace Cr (VI) in environmental samples is of great importance due to its high toxicity. High Cr (VI) absorption in vivo can lead to dermatitis, stomach ulcers and different types of cancer [21]. As chromium is widely used in many industrial processes, there is a high risk of water contamination by Cr (VI) release with the consequent ecological impact. Common analytical techniques for selective determination of Cr (VI) include atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and X-ray fluorescence [22]. Although different types of GQD have been evaluated as electrochemiluminescence [23] and fluorescence sensing probes for Cr (VI) detection [24], to the best of our knowledge this is the first report on the development of reusable GQD-containing filtration membranes as turn-off fluorescence sensing platforms for sensitive and selective determination of Cr (VI) in water. We will show that membrane-confined GQD exhibit comparable fluorescence sensing response as water dispersed ones and the analytical performance of the membrane remains stable after repetitive Cr (VI) detection, storage in buffer solution and filtration tests without the need of reducing or chelating agents to regenerate the fluorescence platforms.

NaCl, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, MnSO4, CrO3 and CoCl2·6H2O purchased from Scharlau. Cd(ClO4)2, FeCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·7H20, CaCl2, AgNO3, Pb(ClO4)2 pyrene, ammonia, hydrazine hydrate, citric acid and Hg(ClO4)2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and FeCl3·6H2O from Panreac. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of Synthesis A10 from Millipore (18 Ω cm) (Massachusetts, USA).

TEM images of GQD on carbon-coated copper grids were taken using a TECNAI G2 20 TWIN (FEI) transmission electron microscope, operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV in a bright-field image mode. AFM images on mica substrates were obtained in a tapping mode at room temperature using a scanning probe microscope (Molecular Imagings PicoScan) equipped with a Nanosensors tips/cantilever, at a resonance frequency of 330 kHz and a spring constant of about 42 N/m with a tip nominal radius lower than 7 nm. FTIR spectra were recorded at room temperature using a Jasco 4100LE spectrophotometer with 4 cm−1 resolution in a wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Raman spectra on Si substrates were measured on a WITEC alpha300 RA Raman spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured on a Jasco V-570 UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer and a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. XPS measurements were performed in a SPECS Sage HR 100 spectrometer with a non-monochromatic X-ray source (Magnesium Kα line of 1253.6 eV energy and a power applied of 250 W and calibrated using the 3d5/2 line of Ag with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 1.1 eV. Measurements were made in an ultra-high vacuum chamber at a pressure around 8 × 10−8 mbar. The selected resolution for the spectra was 30 eV pass energy and 0.5 eV/step for the general survey spectra and 15 eV pass energy and 0.15 eV/step for the detailed spectra of the different elements. In the fittings asymmetric and Gaussian-Lorentzian functions were used (after a Shirley background correction) where the FWHM of all the peaks were constrained while the peak positions and areas were set free.

GQD were synthesized according to a method reported elsewhere [25]. Briefly, in a typical procedure pyrene (1 g) was nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in 65% wt. HNO3 (80 mL) under refluxing and stirring for 16 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with deionized (DI) water and filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane. Part of the resultant 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (1 g) was dispersed by ultrasonication in a DI water solution (0.1 L) containing 0.2 M ammonia and 1.5 M hydrazine hydrate for 1 h. The suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooled to room temperature, the GQD solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane to remove insoluble carbon product, and further dialysed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 1 day to remove salt and small molecules. For comparative purpose, carbon quantum dots (CQD) were also synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid [26].

Nylon (Magna, 0.22 μm pore size), cellulose (Albet, 38–43 μm pore size), transparent cellulose (Aledinha, 0.1 μm pore size), nitrocellulose (Millipore, 5 μm pore size), polypropylene (Millipore, 1.2 μm pore size), alumina (Whatman, 0.2 μm pore size) membranes and glass fiber pads (Millipore) were tested as GQD supports. Membranes were immersed for 10 min in aqueous solutions with different GQD concentrations (0.015–1.6 mg/mL). Afterwards, the membranes were thoroughly rinsed with DI water and dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) and interferents
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence measurements were done in 96-well fluorescence plates, placing small pieces (4 mm × 4 mm) cut from the membranes at the bottom of the wells. Blank measurements were done with the GQD-nylon membrane immersed in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PBS) pH 7. The fluorescence quenching effect of Cr (VI) concentration was investigated in two ways: (i) adding aliquots of Cr (VI) onto a single well containing a piece of membrane immersed in buffer to evaluate intra-membrane sensing response (reproducibility of the detection method), and (ii) placing test solutions with different Cr (VI) concentrations in separate wells each containing a piece of membrane to evaluate inter-membrane sensing response (reproducibility of the membrane GQD coating method). Fluorescence spectra were recorded at a scan rate of 10 nm/s with a 5 nm slot width both for excitation and emission. Fluorescence quenching by the different analytes was measured at the maximum emission wavelength, 496 nm at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. Measurements were done immediately after immersing the membrane in the test solution without any incubation time.

For the immersion stability tests, the GQD-nylon membranes were stored immersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 for several days, rinsed with DI water and dried before measuring fluorescence in buffer and in Cr (VI) solutions. DI water filtration tests through the GQD-nylon membranes were done in a Millipore vacuum filtration system and the fluorescence sensing response was evaluated after filtering different volumes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQD and GQD-nylon membranes
ce:section.ce:para: GQD were synthesized by hydrothermal condensation of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in a mixture of hydrazine hydrate and ammonia according to a protocol reported elsewhere [25]. The method yields GQD functionalized by NHNH2 moiety that exhibit a bright cyan fluorescence. TEM and AFM images (Fig. 1 ) reveal that the GQD are very uniform in terms of lateral size and thickness, with an average size of 2.7 ± 0.4 nm and height of 1.5 ± 0.4 nm, indicating that most of the GQD were composed of 2–5 layers.

Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a) shows the characteristic ordered G and disordered D bands of graphene-like materials at 1587 and 1372 cm−1 respectively. The D to G band intensity ratio (ID/IG = 1.01) indicates the presence of a relative high proportion of defects and edge functional groups in the GQD as a result of their small lateral size. The functional groups in the GQD were identified by FTIR spectroscopy. The main bands in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b) can be assigned to the following vibrations: the CC vibration at 1590 cm−1, the N-H vibration from the NHNH2 moiety at 3216 and 1618 cm−1, the O-H vibration at 3360 cm−1 and the C-OH vibration at 1280 cm−1. That is, the GQD have both amine and hydroxyl groups originating from the precursor and the media (hydrazine and ammonia) used in the condensation reaction. The presence of these functional groups was also corroborated by XPS analysis, as the high-resolution C 1s, O 1s and N 1s spectra revealed the signals from C-O (287.3 eV), C-N (401.2 eV), N-H (399.5 eV) and O-H (532.5 eV) bonds (supporting information, Fig. S1). The chemical composition of the GQD estimated from the XPS survey spectrum indicates 71.1 at.% C, 18.2 at.% O and 10.7 at.% N contents.

The optical properties of GQD were investigated by UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The UV–visible absorption spectrum (black line in Fig. 2c) exhibits three well-defined absorption bands located at 250, 290 and 400 nm, which can be assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains and electronic transitions between energy levels from the surface states arising from the functional groups [2]. In turn, the PL excitation spectrum features three intense bands at 260, 308 and 418 nm, in good agreement with the corresponding UV–vis absorption bands. The most intense PL emission, centered at 496 nm, is obtained with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. However, the PL emission is excitation-dependent and when the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 to 460 nm the PL emission peak slightly shifts to longer wavelengths (from 475 to 505 nm) and at long excitation wavelengths the PL intensity decreases sharply (Fig. 2d). This excitation-dependent PL emission has been commonly observed in GQD and has been ascribed to excitation of different sized GQD or conjugated sp2 domains in the GQD carbon network [27].

After characterizing GQD solutions, successful immobilization of GQD in the nylon membranes was corroborated by PL measurements of the GQD-modified membranes. Interestingly, GQD PL emission did not vary after immobilization in the nylon membranes, as can be seen in the normalized PL emission spectra compared in Fig. 3 a. Both dispersed and membrane-confined GQD exhibit the brightest PL emission at 496 nm when excited at 420 nm and the same excitation-dependent PL emission is noted in the GQD-nylon membranes (supporting information, Fig. S2). The inset in Fig. 3a and b shows photographs of GQD aqueous solutions and GQD-nylon membranes exposed to visible and UV light (365 nm). Dilute GQD solutions (0.015 mg/mL) are pale yellow and exhibit bright cyan fluorescence under UV irradiation. In turn, GQD retained in nylon membranes at low dosage hardly change the membrane white color but the GQD-modified membranes exhibit very bright cyan fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 3b, inset).

As described in the experimental section, GQD immobilization in the membrane was simply achieved by immersion of the membranes in GQD aqueous solutions. Despite its simplicity, the method is versatile in terms of GQD content as it was possible to vary the amount of GQD retained in the membranes, and consequently their fluorescence intensity, by simply increasing GQD concentration of the bathing solutions. Photographs of GQD-nylon membranes obtained after immersion in GQD solutions of different concentrations under visible and UV light are shown in Fig. 3b. Despite thorough rinsing and immersion of GQD-nylon membranes in DI water for some minutes in all cases, it is clear that immobilized GQD content in the membranes increased with their concentration in the bathing solution as indicated by the increasingly yellow color of the membrane. Nevertheless, the PL emission intensity does not scale with GQD content in the membrane. As shown in Fig. 3b the most emissive GQD-nylon membrane was prepared from a 0.028 mg/mL GQD solution with PL intensity decaying at higher GQD contents. In the case of GQD dispersed in aqueous solutions the highest PL intensity is typically achieved at a concentration of 0.015 mg/mL.

The versatility of GQD immobilization method was tested with membranes of different materials and pore sizes, namely: opaque cellulose (38–43 μm), transparent cellulose (0.1 μm), nitrocellulose (5 μm), polypropylene (1.2 μm), alumina (0.2 μm) and glass fiber absorbent pads. Fig. 3c shows photographs under natural and UV light of those membranes where GQD immobilization was successful, specifically: nylon, cellulose and glass fiber. Overall, these results indicate that the extent of GQD immobilization is mostly influenced by membrane composition while pore size does not have a significant effect. Thus, GQD were successfully and stably immobilized in the two types of cellulose membranes irrespective of their pore size (small −0.1 μm- and large −40 μm-) whereas immobilization failed in nitrocellulose (5 μm), alumina (0.2 μm) and polypropylene (1.2 μm), as indicated by absence of luminescence. Therefore, GQD immobilization cannot be ascribed to simple physical entrapment and they are most likely bonded by weak chemical interactions such as hydrogen bonds (GQD have abundant NHNH2 and OH groups) to the membrane material (cellulose, nylon and glass).

The PL response of GQD-modified membranes in 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 7 solutions with different Cr (VI) concentrations was measured in 96-well fluorescence plates, placing small pieces cut from the membranes at the bottom of the wells (supporting information, Fig. S3). As each piece will be used to determine one Cr (VI) concentration, it is crucial to ensure the reproducibility of GQD content within a membrane. PL intensity at 496 nm was measured in different points of the dry membrane and the relative standard deviation (R.S.D., n = 10) was 5%. Nevertheless, the PL sensing performance of GQD-modified membranes was investigated both by adding increasing aliquots of Cr (VI) onto a piece in a single well (single-piece test) and measuring solutions with different Cr (VI) concentrations in separate wells each containing a piece of membrane (multiple-piece test). The two testing methods will provide separate information on the robustness of a GQD-membrane as PL sensing platform and on the reproducibility of the GQD-membrane preparation method.

Prior to evaluating the performance of GQD-modified membranes as PL sensing platforms for Cr (VI) detection, we explored the optimal GQD content for analytical purposes by measuring PL intensity changes of nylon membranes with different GQD content immersed in 100 μM Cr (VI) and 0.1 M PBS solution pH 7. As can be seen in Fig. 3b, the most pronounced PL emission quenching (F/F0, where F and F0 are PL intensities at 496 nm after and before Cr (VI) addition) corresponds to the GQD-nylon membrane with highest PL emission prepared from a 0.028 mg/mL GQD solution. Therefore, unless otherwise stated, membranes used throughout the work have been prepared in these conditions.

Fig. 4 a shows typical PL emission spectra of a piece of GQD-nylon membrane immersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 recorded upon addition of aliquots of Cr (VI) solution (single piece test). The PL intensity of the GQD-nylon membrane decreases gradually with increasing Cr (VI) concentration, demonstrating the feasibility of the modified nylon membrane as fluorescence detection platform. The sensing performance of other types of GQD-modified membranes has also been evaluated, and no significant differences with nylon supports have been noted (supporting information, table S1). In order to confirm that detection occurs at the membrane and not in solution due to GQD release, the piece of membrane was removed from the test well and no fluorescence was detected. On the contrary, if the tested piece of membrane was placed in a different well, the initial PL intensity was recovered.

Additionally, the feasibility of Cr (VI) detection using different pieces of membrane and Cr (VI) concentrations in each well was also investigated (multiple-piece test). As in the single-piece detection test, decreasing PL intensities with increasing Cr (VI) concentration were measured as a result of PL quenching of GQD-modified membranes by Cr (VI) ions, as shown in the photograph of the 96-well fluorescence plate under UV light in Fig. 4a. The mechanism of PL quenching has been ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer from GQD to Cr (VI) with consequent reduction to Cr (III), as corroborated by XPS investigation of the oxidation state of GQD and adsorbed Cr (III) after the reaction between GQD and Cr (VI) [24]. Later on, other authors have studied in-depth the mechanism of GQD PL quenching by Cr (VI) and ascribed it to a combined static quenching and inner filter effect [28]. The former results from the ground state recombination between GQD and Cr (VI) whereas the latter consists in absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by Cr (VI), whose absorption bands cover the excitation and emission bands of GQD.

Fig 4b shows the linear response of fluorescence intensity changes in GQD-modified membranes with Cr (VI) concentration obtained both for single-piece and multiple-piece tests. For comparative purposes, Cr (VI) detection has also been done using GQD as fluorescence probes dispersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 aqueous solutions and the corresponding calibration plot is also shown in Fig 4b. In all cases fluorescence change values for each concentration are the average of three replicates. In the three individual tests, a significant linear correlation exists between the values of (F0-F)/F at 496 nm and Cr (VI) concentration in the range of 1–500 μM, which is comparable or slightly higher to linear ranges reported for other dispersed GQD fluorescent probes [24,27]. In the three fluorescence quenching tests the values could be fitted to the Stern-Volmer equation (F0-F)/F = KSV × [Cr (VI)] + B, yielding the following Stern-Volmer constants and correlation coefficient values: KSV = 5,25 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9897, n = 14) for single-piece tests, KSV = 5,07 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9884, n = 12) for multiple-piece tests and KSV = 4,81 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9978, n = 16) for dispersed GQD tests. Overall, the Stern-Volmer constants are also in agreement with values previously reported for dispersed GQD probes and highlight the excellent performance of GQD-modified membranes as turn-off fluorescence sensing platforms.

While the three detection methods yield comparable linear concentration range and sensitivity, there are more significant differences in the detection limit, which arise from the reproducibility of each method. Thus, the detection limits for Cr (VI), calculated as 3 σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank response and K is the slope of the calibration plot) for each detection protocol were: 190 nM for single-piece tests, 170 nM for multiple-piece tests and 20 nM for dispersed GQD tests. The reproducibility of each detection method, evaluated as the relative standard deviation (R.S.D.) in the detection of 80 μM Cr (VI) (n = 5) was 2.4% for single-piece tests, 2.2% for multiple-piece-tests and 0.27% for dispersed GQD tests. Despite the differences, the detection limit in all cases is well below the maximum level (1 μM) accepted in drinking water by the World Health Organization [29]. We also interrogated amorphous (non sp2-based) carbon quantum dots (CQD) as fluorescent probes for Cr (VI) detection. Although Cr (VI)-induced PL quenching was noted for CQD solutions, intensity change and hence sensitivity was much lower than those for GQD solutions (supporting information, Fig. S4). Moreover, PL of CQD-modified membranes was negligible compared to that of GQD-based ones and consequently insufficient to resolve changes induced by Cr (VI) (supporting information, Fig. S4). Altogether, amorphous CQD did not seem as good building block as GQD for turn-off fluorescent sensing platforms.

Next, the selectivity of GQD-nylon membranes for Cr (VI) detection was evaluated measuring PL intensity changes of several membrane pieces in presence of potentially interfering metal ions: Na+, K+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and Ag+. Fig. 5 shows average (n = 3) quenching values (F/F0, where F and F0 are fluorescence values in presence and absence of each metal ion) for a GQD-nylon membrane in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 solution. As can be seen, PL quenching by most tested cations is negligible compared with that induced by Cr (VI), highlighting the high selectivity of GQD-nylon membranes for Cr (VI) detection. Selectivity arises from the high binding affinity and fast chelating kinetics of Cr (VI) with NHNH2 and OH functional groups present in the GQD, as demonstrated by previous studies comparing N-doped and undoped GQD as soluble fluorescent probes where undoped GQD showed much lower selectivity towards Cr (VI) than N-GQD counterparts [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage and operational stability of GQD-modified membranes
ce:section.ce:para: After demonstrating the viability of GQD-modified membranes as fluorescence sensing platforms for sensitive and selective Cr (VI) detection, membrane reusability was evaluated. A same piece of membrane was used repeatedly 25 consecutive times to measure PL quenching in 0.1 M PBS solution with high Cr (VI) concentration, 500 μM. The membrane was only rinsed with DI water between the sensing tests, without adding a reducing or chelating agent for membrane regeneration. Reusability measurements were replicated three times with three different membranes. The repeatability of the PL quenching response (F/F0 = 0.285 ± 0.014) was very high, with a R.S.D of 5% (n = 25) and no apparent trend with the number of uses (supporting information, Fig. S5).

GQD-modified membranes were also very stable upon storage immersed in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 (supporting information, Fig. S6). The PL quenching response of GQD-nylon membranes in PBS solutions with different Cr (VI) concentrations after varying immersion times are compared in Fig. 6 a. Plotted (F0-F)/F values for each immersion time are the average of two independent measurements done with two membranes. As can be seen, the PL quenching response of the different membranes has good linearity with Cr (VI) concentration in the range 50–400 μM and the sensitivity is not significantly altered with immersion time. Thus, fitting the PL quenching values from the calibration plot to the Stern-Volmer equation, the following slopes were obtained for the different immersion times: KSV = 5,25 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9897) for the as-prepared membrane, KSV = 5,50 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9952) for 4-day and KSV = 4,98 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9952) for 7-day immersion time proving the excellent time stability of wetted GQD-nylon membranes. In contrast, the stability of 7-day wetted GQD-cellulose membranes sensing response to a 225 μM Cr (VI) solution was comparatively lower and GQD-glass fiber pads lost their sensing capability after 7-days storage in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 (supporting information, table S1).

Finally, stability of the sensing response of GQD-nylon membranes after filtering different volumes of DI water was investigated. Interestingly, filtration flux does not vary significantly in GQD-modified membranes compared to pristine ones. Fig. 6b compares PL intensity quenching in 0.1 M PBS pH 7 with different Cr (VI) concentrations for membranes after filtering different volumes of DI water. Plotted (F0-F)/F values for each filtered volume are the average of three measurements done with different membranes. Regardless of the filtered volume, PL intensity quenching of the membranes exhibits a good linear response with Cr (VI) concentration in the range 50–400 μM with negligible changes in the slope of the linear regression. Fitting PL quenching values to the Stern-Volmer equation yields the following constants for the different filtered volumes: KSV = 4,89 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9869) for the as-prepared membrane, KSV = 3,98 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9971) for 0.25 L, KSV = 4,68 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9966) for 0.50 L and KSV = 4,67 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.9863) for 1 L volume. That is, there are no large differences or a clear trend in the slopes, demonstrating the good operational stability of the membranes as fluorescence sensing platforms under filtration tests.

We have presented a simple, versatile and reproducible method to produce filtration membranes incorporating GQD fluorescent probes for fast, sensitive and selective detection of highly toxic Cr (VI) ions in water. When confined to membrane, the GQD fluorescence fingerprint and the quenching effect of Cr (VI) were preserved. Thus, we have proven that GQD-modified membranes are very sensitive, efficient, durable and reusable detection platforms for pollutants of high industrial and environmental relevance. Compared with the use of dispersed GQD as fluorescence detection probes, integration of the sensing nanoblocks in a reusable filtration membrane allows analyte detection under operational conditions. We envisage that further versatility in terms of membrane nature and target analyte could be tuned by tailoring GQD functionalization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Basque Government under the ELKARTEK Program (ACTIMAT project, grant number: KK-2015/00094) and Diputación Foral de Gipuzkoa (BIOGRAF project, grant number: 32/15) is gratefully acknowledged. We wish to thank Dr. Graciela Martinez Paredes for fruitful discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.08.038.
